summary: O'Neil highlights two articles exposing deep flaws in standardized testing: one showing how poor schools can't compete because tests are tied to expensive textbooks (with Pearson caught using verbatim textbook passages on tests), and another examining how Atlanta's high-stakes testing culture predictably led to widespread cheating. She argues that the system creates two tiers of 'cheating'—wealthy schools buying their way to success through test-prep materials, and desperate teachers changing answers—while the testing regime functions more as punishment than education. She notes Christie's decision to reduce test score weight in teacher evaluations as a small positive development.
key_points: Standardized tests are designed to favor schools that can afford expensive textbooks from the same companies that create the tests
Pearson was caught using passages from their own textbooks verbatim on standardized tests, creating a pay-to-win system
Atlanta's cheating scandal was a predictable consequence of high-stakes 'data-driven' education policy
Data was weaponized to 'embarrass and punish' rather than to help students and teachers
There's a class-based double standard: buying expensive test prep is acceptable 'cheating' while changing answers is criminal
money_quotes: Pearson came under fire last year for using a passage on a standardized test that was taken verbatim from a Pearson textbook.
A 'culture of fear, intimidation and retaliation has infested the district, allowing cheating—at all levels—to go unchecked for years.'
Data had been 'used as an abusive and cruel weapon to embarrass and punish.'

There's an acceptable way to cheat, which is by stocking up on expensive test prep materials in the form of testing company-sponsored textbooks, and then there's the unacceptable way to cheat, which is where teachers change the answers.
Either way the standardized test scoring regime comes out looking like a penal system rather than a helpful teaching aid.
key_insight: Standardized testing creates a rigged system where wealth buys 'legitimate' advantages while punishing those who can't afford to play by the rules designed to benefit test-makers' profits.
